feed.title: SAP Community - Joule
feed.subtitle: Joule blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: SAP MDG News: AI Use Cases delivered, demoed and presented
entry.content:
Last week, I had the opportunity to present the latest SAP MDG and AI developments to a major client. Since the 2023 release, SAP MDG has unlocked numerous AI use cases, with most integrated directly into Joule and additional embedded AI features available. The live demo showcased all these capabilities in action.

Key AI Use Cases Overview

We're currently seeing three interaction types of Joule with MDG that are transforming how users work with master data:

Use Case 1: Informational

Knowledge at Your Fingertips: With Joule integrated into the SAP Launchpad, business users can ask MDG-related questions without leaving their current workspace. Instead of opening external browsers or applications, users can simply ask questions like "Does MDG support parallel processes for the same object?" Joule accesses the complete SAP Help documentation and SAP Community to deliver high-quality answers instantly.

Use Case 2: Navigational

Smart Data Discovery: Users can pose specific questions about master data within their MDG system. For example, asking "Show me all Business Partners with NWT in their name" displays results directly within the Joule chatbot. Users can then navigate directly from Joule to the appropriate app or action, eliminating the traditional process of opening the correct app, searching for business partners, and then proceeding with tasks.

 

Use Case 3: Transactional

Guided Process Creation: This powerful capability is already available in MDG cloud ready mode. When creating a new Business Partner via Joule, users interact within the chatbot interface, receiving guided assistance with the right questions for BP creation. The system also provides duplicate detection alerts directly within the chat experience

Custom AI Solutions Discussion

Beyond standard use cases, we explored customer-specific implementations using SAP Business AI to enhance master data quality. The concept of an "Attribute Recommendation Agent" could automatically improve data quality through regular evaluation and remediation processes, keeping master data current and accurate through automated quality checks.

Client Feedback Section

The feedback on delivered use cases was very positive! We also discussed exciting possibilities for implementing custom AI use cases with SAP Business AI to further enhance their master data governance processes.

Call to Action

For more detailed insights, check out this comprehensive blog: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/meet-joule-your-ai-copilot-for-sap-master-data-governance/ba-p/14273811

Meet Joule - Your AI Copilot for SAP Master Data Governance 🤖

Best regards,

Steffen

entry.title: Building and securing MCP servers in Python on BTP
entry.content:
MCP (Model Context Protocol) is the standard for tool access in AI agents. As SAP developers build extensions and integrations on SAP BTP, creating MCP servers has become equally relevant to expose business functionalities to AI agents. Whether you're extending Joule Studio or building custom AI solutions, MCP servers provide a standardized way to make your BTP services AI-accessible. In this tutorial, I'll show you how to build a production-ready MCP server on SAP Cloud Foundry using Python.

Scenario

We'll create three progressively complex MCP servers:
1. Simple Math Server - Understanding the basics
2. Research Server - Integrating Perplexity AI via Generative AI Hub
3. Secured Research Server - Adding IAS authentication

All running on SAP Cloud Foundry, one of the most flexible and cost-effective runtime options on BTP.

Prerequisites

Before starting, ensure you have:
- SAP BTP account with Cloud Foundry environment
- Cloud Foundry CLI installed and configured
- Python 3.9+ installed locally
- SAP Generative AI Hub access with service key (for Perplexity example)
- SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) tenant with admin access (for authentication example)

Example 1: Simple MCP Server

Let's start with the basics. We'll use the fastmcp library which handles MCP's protocol specifications elegantly.

from starlette.responses import JSONResponse
from fastmcp import FastMCP
import os

# Initialize FastMCP server
mcp = FastMCP("Simple Math Server")


@mcp.tool()
def add(a: float, b: float) -> float:
    """
    Add two numbers together.

    Args:
        a: First number
        b: Second number

    Returns:
        Sum of a and b
    """
    return a + b


if __name__ == "__main__":
    mcp.run(transport="http", host="0.0.0.0", port=int(os.environ.get("PORT", 8080)))

Key BTP Adaptations:
- PORT environment variable: Cloud Foundry assigns a dynamic port via the PORT environment variable — we must use it
- HTTP transport: We use HTTP transport (not stdio) to make the MCP server accessible via network

To deploy, we need the manifest.yaml and a requirements.txt file. In addition I like to use a runtime.txt to specify a later python version.

---
applications:
- name: simple-mcp-server
  memory: 1024M
  disk_quota: 1024M
  buildpack: python_buildpack
  command: python server.py

Deploy to Cloud Foundry:

cd mcp_simple
cf push

That's it! You now have a working MCP server on BTP.

Example 2: Research Server with Perplexity AI

Now let's build something more practical: a research tool powered by Perplexity AI through SAP Generative AI Hub.

Why wrap Perplexity instead of using it directly?

You might wonder why not use Perplexity's native MCP server. Two reasons:
1. Educational value - Great exercise to understand the full stack
2. Generative AI Hub integration - The Hub exposes the API but not API keys. Perplexity's native MCP requires direct API keys, which isn't compatible with the Hub's proxy model where credentials stay on the server side.

Perplexity Client Implementation

This client handles:
- Loading AI Core credentials from ai_core_key.json
- OAuth token management with automatic refresh
- Calling Perplexity's sonar model via Generative AI Hub
- Formatting results with citations and related questions

Here's the core implementation snippet showing the Gen AI Hub integration:

class PerplexityClient:
    """Client for Perplexity API via SAP Gen AI Hub native proxy."""

    def __init__(self, config_file: str = 'ai_core_key.json'):
        """Initialize the client with AI Core credentials."""
        self.config = self._load_config(config_file)
        self.token = None
        self.token_expiry = 0
        self._refresh_token()

        # IMPORTANT: Create a Gen AI Hub deployment for Perplexity's sonar-pro model
        # Replace this with your own deployment ID from AI Core
        self.deployment_id = "d0229dc9c4b56704"

        # Build API URL: Gen AI Hub base URL + deployment endpoint
        self.base_url = self.config['serviceurls']['AI_API_URL']
        self.api_url = f"{self.base_url}/v2/inference/deployments/{self.deployment_id}/chat/completions"

    def research(self, query: str) -> str:
        """Research a query using Perplexity AI with real-time web search."""
        self._ensure_valid_token()

        payload = {
            "model": "sonar-pro",
            "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": query}],
            "max_tokens": 5000,
            "temperature": 0.3,
            "return_citations": True
        }

        headers = {
            "Authorization": f"Bearer {self.token}",
            "Content-Type": "application/json",
            "AI-Resource-Group": "default"
        }

        response = requests.post(self.api_url, json=payload, headers=headers)
        # ... format results with citations ...

 

The key is the URL structure: {AI_API_URL}/v2/inference/deployments/{deployment_id}/chat/completions — this routes through Gen AI Hub to Perplexity's native API while preserving citation metadata.

Note: You'll need to create a deployment in AI Core for the Perplexity model and update the deployment_id in the code with your own.

MCP Server with Research Tool

from starlette.responses import JSONResponse
from fastmcp import FastMCP
import os
from perplexity import PerplexityClient

# Initialize FastMCP server
mcp = FastMCP("Research Server")

# Initialize Perplexity client
client = PerplexityClient()


@mcp.tool()
def research(query: str) -> str:
    """
    Research tool using Perplexity AI with real-time web search and citations.

    Args:
        query: The research query (simple string input)

    Returns:
        Research results with answer and source citations
    """
    return client.research(query)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    mcp.run(transport="http", host="0.0.0.0", port=int(os.environ.get("PORT", 8080)))

Simple and clean — the MCP decorator handles all protocol complexity, and we just delegate the query to our Perplexity client.

Important: We're using Generative AI Hub's native Perplexity proxy (not the orchestration module) because it preserves citation metadata that's crucial for research tasks.

Example 3: Adding IAS Authentication

For production use, we need to secure the MCP server. Let's add authentication using SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS).

IAS Setup Prerequisites

Before implementing authentication, complete these IAS configuration steps:
1. Create an IAS application in your IAS tenant
2. Configure OAuth 2.0 client credentials
3. Add custom attribute ias_apis with value api_read_access
4. Note your token URL, client ID, client secret, and audience

For detailed IAS setup instructions, refer to my previous blog post on Python authentication with IAS.

Secured MCP Server Implementation

The secured version adds an authentication middleware that:
- Validates JWT tokens from IAS
- Verifies token signature using JWKS (JSON Web Key Set)
- Checks audience and issuer claims
- Ensures required scope (api_read_access) is present

from fastmcp import FastMCP
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
import os
import requests
import jwt
import uvicorn
from perplexity import PerplexityClient
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware

# Initialize Perplexity client
client = PerplexityClient()

# Create MCP server
mcp = FastMCP("Research Server")

# IAS Configuration - Replace with your values
ISSUER = "https://your-tenant.accounts.ondemand.com"
JWKS_URL = f"{ISSUER}/oauth2/certs"
AUDIENCE = "your-client-id-here"


class IASAuthMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    """Middleware to validate IAS JWT tokens."""

    def get_public_key(self, token: str):
        """Fetch JWKS and find matching public key for token validation."""
        kid = jwt.get_unverified_header(token)["kid"]
        jwks = requests.get(JWKS_URL).json()

        for key in jwks["keys"]:
            if key["kid"] == kid:
                return jwt.algorithms.RSAAlgorithm.from_jwk(key)

        raise Exception("No matching key found in JWKS")

    def verify_token(self, token: str):
        """Validate JWT token signature and claims."""
        public_key = self.get_public_key(token)
        payload = jwt.decode(
            token,
            public_key,
            algorithms=["RS256"],
            audience=AUDIENCE,
            issuer=ISSUER
        )

        # Verify required scope
        if "api_read_access" not in payload.get("ias_apis", []):
            raise Exception("Missing required ias_apis scope")

        return payload

    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next):
        # Extract and validate authorization header
        auth_header = request.headers.get("Authorization")

        if not auth_header or not auth_header.startswith("Bearer "):
            return JSONResponse(
                status_code=401,
                content={"detail": "Missing or invalid authorization header"}
            )

        token = auth_header.split(" ")[1]

        try:
            payload = self.verify_token(token)
            request.state.user = payload
        except Exception as e:
            return JSONResponse(
                status_code=401,
                content={"detail": f"Authentication failed: {str(e)}"}
            )

        return await call_next(request)


@mcp.tool()
def research(query: str) -> str:
    """
    Research tool using Perplexity AI with real-time web search and citations.

    Args:
        query: The research query

    Returns:
        Research results with answer and source citations
    """
    return client.research(query)


# Create ASGI app from MCP server
mcp_app = mcp.http_app(path='/mcp')

# Create FastAPI wrapper with lifespan management
app = FastAPI(title="Researcher MCP", lifespan=mcp_app.lifespan)

# Add IAS authentication middleware
mcp_app.add_middleware(IASAuthMiddleware)

# Mount the MCP server
app.mount("/", mcp_app)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=int(os.environ.get("PORT", 8080)))

What changed for authentication:

The key difference from the unsecured version is the middleware layer. Since fastmcp is built on Starlette/FastAPI, we can leverage standard ASGI middleware patterns. The IASAuthMiddleware intercepts every request, validates the JWT token against IAS's public keys (JWKS), and checks required claims before allowing access to MCP endpoints.

To integrate the middleware, we extract the ASGI app from fastmcp using http_app(), wrap it with FastAPI to manage lifespan properly, add our authentication middleware, and mount it. This pattern works seamlessly with Cloud Foundry's router.

Testing Authentication

Test your secured endpoint:

1. Without token (should fail):
curl "https://research-mcp-server-ias-auth.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com/mcp"
Response: 401 - {"detail":"Missing or invalid authorization header"}

2. With valid token (should succeed):
curl "https://research-mcp-server-ias-auth.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com/mcp" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer eyJqa3U..."
Response: 200 OK with mcp-session-id header

Perfect! Our MCP server now requires valid IAS authentication.

Using the MCP Server in Joule Studio

Now that we have a secured MCP server, let's integrate it with Joule Studio to build an AI agent with research capabilities.

Step 1: Create a Destination

Navigate to your BTP Subaccount (where Joule Studio is running):
1. Go to Connectivity → Destinations
2. Create a new destination with these settings:
- Name: research-mcp-server
- Type: HTTP
- URL: https://research-mcp-server-ias-auth.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com
- Authentication: OAuth2ClientCredentials
- Client ID: Your IAS client ID
- Client Secret: Your IAS client secret
- Token Service URL: https://your-tenant.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/token

Additional Properties are needed for Joule Studio to recognize the Destination accordingly:

sap-joule-studio-mcp-server: true
sap.processautomation.enabled: true

Important: Don't include /mcp in the URL — Joule Studio appends this automatically.

Now the Destination again needs to be added to the respective environment of SAP Build Process Automation via the Control Tower > Destinations.


Step 2: Create Agent in Joule Studio

 

1. Create a new Agent in your Joule Extension Project
2. Define the agent's expertise and instructions
3. In the MCP section, click Add MCP Server
4. Select your research-mcp-server destination
5. Verify the /mcp path (default is fine)
6. You'll see the available tools (in our case: research)

 


Step 3: Test Your Agent

Start a conversation with your agent and ask it to research current information:

"Research the current situation in the Middle East"

The agent now has access to real-time web information via your MCP server!

 


This opens powerful possibilities: combine up-to-date web data with transactional business data from S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, or other SAP systems.

Using It in Action

Now let's test it in action - I started the agent and asked it to research the current situation in the Middle East and it works! Up-to-date information directly in Joule - can now be combined with any business transactional data one might pull from an S/4HANA system.

By the way, check out my blog on connecting Joule Studio to on-premises HTTP APIs to extend this pattern further.

In the GitHub repository I've added all the different examples including the simple example, with auth based on IAS as well as based on XSUAA, and the Perplexity code. Hope you found this interesting. Leave a comment in case of any questions!

entry.title: Extending SAP Joule with Custom DAS Capabilities
entry.content:
SAP Joule's Digital Assistant Service (DAS) enables extending Joule with custom capabilities: custom scenarios, functions, and backend integrations that respond to natural language queries. On paper, it sounds straightforward: define the scenarios in YAML, compile, deploy, and Joule starts answering domain-specific questions.

In practice, getting a custom DAS capability to work end-to-end: from a user's question in Work Zone all the way to a backend API response: involves navigating a chain of components across multiple subaccounts, each with its own quirks and undocumented constraints.

This post captures the real issues we hit while building a custom Joule integration and the fixes that got us through.

The Architecture: What we are Building

Before diving into the issues, it helps to understand the full request flow. When a user asks Joule a question, the request traverses multiple layers:

User (Work Zone / Standalone Joule)
        │
        ▼
DAS Runtime (Joule subaccount)
  1. Preselection → matches the scenario via semantic similarity
  2. Executes the DAS function
  3. Resolves destination
  4. POSTs to the backend API
        │
        ▼  via destination (OAuth2ClientCredentials)
Backend Service (application subaccount)
  1. Parses request, resolves entity references
  2. Processes business logic
  3. Calls downstream services (AI, DB, etc.)
        │
        ▼
Downstream Service
  Processes and returns response

Each arrow in this diagram is a potential failure point. The DAS runtime, the backend, and any downstream services live in different subaccounts with different networking and authentication contexts. This is where things get interesting.

Issue 1: DAS Compilation: The Hidden Limits

joule compile has strict validation rules that aren't always obvious.

Timeout ceiling: The api-request action type has a maximum timeout of 45 seconds. Set it to 60 and compilation fails silently with a generic validation error. If the backend needs more time, the API needs optimization rather than a longer timeout.

Description length limit: The response_context description fields in scenario YAMLs have a 50 character limit. This is easy to exceed when trying to be descriptive:

# This will fail compilation: too long
- value: $target_result.currentContent
  description: The current content of the section before the proposed edit is applied

# This works: under 50 characters
- value: $target_result.currentContent
  description: Current content before the edit

Issue 2: "I Don't Understand": When Preselection Ignores the Scenario

This was the most ambiguous issue. Everything compiled and deployed successfully, but Joule kept responding with "I don't understand" or routing queries to SAP Help instead of our custom scenario.

The root cause: DAS preselection uses semantic similarity to match a user's query against scenario and capability descriptions. If the descriptions are too generic, they won't score high enough to beat the built-in capabilities like smalltalkCapability.

How to diagnose: Check the Joule debug logs and look for RAGE_SEARCHFILTER / RESULTS_FOUND. If only smalltalkCapability and px_user_feedback_capability appear in the results, the scenario isn't making it past preselection.

The fix: Enrich the descriptions with domain-specific keywords that users would naturally say:

# Too generic: loses to built-in capabilities
description: Answers questions about a document.

# Keyword-rich: wins preselection
description: >-
  Answers questions about project documents including requirements,
  architecture, data model, interfaces, personas, security,
  assumptions, and scope. Use when the user asks about project
  details, specifications, or design decisions.

Important: Update both the scenario YAML and capability.sapdas.yaml descriptions. Preselection checks both levels.

Issue 3: "Destination Not Found": The Cross-Subaccount Trap

W_API_REQUEST_EXECUTION errors with "destination not found."

The destination existed. We could see it in the BTP cockpit. But DAS couldn't find it.

The key insight: DAS runtime resolves destinations from its own subaccount, not from the application's subaccount. If the destination was only created in the app's subaccount, DAS will never see it.

The fix is twofold:

  1. Create a Destination Service instance (lite plan) in the Joule subaccount if one doesn't exist
  2. Create the destination in the Joule subaccount (or both subaccounts)

The destination configuration needs:

Property Value

NameThe destination name
TypeHTTP
URLThe backend's public URL
ProxyTypeInternet
AuthenticationOAuth2ClientCredentials
Client IDFrom XSUAA service key
Client SecretFrom XSUAA service key
Token Service URLThe subdomain's authentication token endpoint

Issue 4: The .internal Route Problem

Even with the destination correctly configured, we got 404 Not Found with x-cf-routererror: unknown_route.

The culprit: MTA deploys Cloud Foundry apps with .internal.cfapps. routes by default. These internal routes are not reachable across subaccounts. Since DAS runs in a different subaccount than the backend, it simply cannot reach an internal route.

Quick fix: map a public route:

cf map-route <app-name>-srv cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com \
  --hostname <app-hostname>

Verify it's reachable (a 401 means reachable but requires auth: that's correct):

curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" \
  "https://<hostname>.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/<api-path>/$metadata"

Then update the destination URL to use the public route (without .internal).

Issue 5: Routes Disappearing After Redeploy

We fixed the route, everything worked, and then after the next cf deploy: Joule broke again with the same 404 unknown_route.

The reason: cf deploy resets routes to only those defined in mta.yaml. Our manually mapped public route was wiped out.

The permanent fix: add the public route to mta.yaml:

- name: <app-name>-srv
  type: nodejs
  path: gen/srv
  parameters:
    routes:
    - route: ${default-host}.internal.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
    - route: ${default-host}.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com   # add this

This ensures the public route survives every deployment.

Issue 6: Works Standalone, Fails in Work Zone

The final issue was the most confusing. Our capability worked perfectly in standalone Joule (/webclient/standalone/<bot-name>), but embedded Joule in SAP Build Work Zone couldn't find any of our scenarios.

The root cause: Work Zone uses the sap_digital_assistant assistant: a system-managed assistant that SAP products (Work Zone, SuccessFactors, etc.) share. Custom assistants created via joule deploy are completely separate.

Diagnosis: Run joule get sap_digital_assistant and check if the capability is listed. If it only shows smalltalkCapability and px_user_feedback_capability, the capability isn't registered.

The fix:

# Register the capability with the Work Zone assistant
joule update sap_digital_assistant --capability-file core/capability.sapdas.yaml

# Verify it's registered
joule get sap_digital_assistant

This was a critical "aha" moment: deploying to a custom assistant is great for testing, but for production use via Work Zone, explicit registration with sap_digital_assistant is required.

The Subaccount Mental Model

One of the biggest sources of confusion was understanding which component lives where. Here's the mapping that finally made everything click:

Component Subaccount Purpose

Backend Service (CAP app)Application subaccountBackend API + DB
Downstream ServicesApplication subaccountAI, processing, etc.
DAS / Joule RuntimeJoule subaccountCapability hosting
Build Work ZoneApplication subaccountUI / Launchpad
Backend DestinationBoth subaccountsDAS → backend connectivity
SWZ_JOULE DestinationApplication subaccountWork Zone → DAS connection

The key takeaway: destinations must exist where they're consumed, not where the target lives. DAS consumes the backend destination, so it must exist in the Joule subaccount.

Essential Commands Reference

For anyone working with DAS, these are the commands needed daily:

# Compile the capability (catches YAML errors early)
joule compile core

# Deploy to a standalone assistant for testing
joule deploy -c -n <bot-name>

# Register with Work Zone's assistant for production
joule update sap_digital_assistant --capability-file core/capability.sapdas.yaml

# List all assistants and inspect them
joule list
joule get sap_digital_assistant
joule get <bot-name>

# Launch standalone Joule for quick testing
joule launch <bot-name>

# Check and manage routes
cf app <app-name>-srv | grep routes
cf map-route <app-name>-srv cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com --hostname <hostname>

Lessons Learned

  • Compile early, compile often. DAS has strict but poorly documented validation rules. Don't wait until deployment to find out the descriptions are too long.
  • Think in subaccounts. Every cross-subaccount interaction: destinations, routes, assistant registration: is a potential failure point. Map out which components live where before starting.
  • Preselection is semantic, not exact. Scenario descriptions are effectively marketing copy for an AI classifier. They should be optimized like search keywords: domain-specific and descriptive.
  • Internal routes are invisible across subaccounts. Always add public routes to mta.yaml to survive redeployments.
  • Standalone ≠ Work Zone. Testing in standalone Joule doesn't validate the Work Zone integration. Always test in both contexts.
  • Debug logs are essential. The RAGE_SEARCHFILTER and RESULTS_FOUND entries reveal exactly what preselection is seeing.

Conclusion

Building a custom SAP Joule DAS integration involves more moving parts. The chain from user query to backend response crosses subaccount boundaries, networking layers, and semantic matching: each with its own failure modes.

The issues we encountered: compilation limits, preselection tuning, cross-subaccount destinations, internal routing, route persistence, and assistant registration, are not edge cases. They're the standard path anyone extending Joule will walk.

entry.title: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the S/4HANA Public Edition (Pt D: Improvements)
entry.content:
Introduction

Now Artificial Intelligence (AI) is on every professional’s mind. Everybody wants to be on the bandwagon.  I plan to write a series of blogs on this topic under a big umbrella “My AI Experience Sharing”.  This is the first mini-series: setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. 

I purposely delayed my action on setting up a Joule to wait for the dust settling down during the Early Adopter phase. The intention paid off but still won’t give me a free pass 😉. There are five credible references available as listed in the Reference section, but none of them give you a good explanation on Why, especially on What in a critical step with a readable picture when you desperately need a hint.  To help all the people like me, this blog will focus on explaining those steps, what to enter, and possible mistakes to avoid. 

This week, I re-did the Joule Setup on a Parallel Line of the SAP S/4HANA ERP Public Edition, aka Public Cloud. I noticed a few changes and would like to outline here for your reference:

You can install Joule on your Parallel Line

Several weeks ago, I met one of our customers at the Customer Success Day in NYC. After listening to his rational behind requiring Joule on the Parallel Line, I promised to do a test. I did just that this week on a Parallel Line. It worked.

No need to NavigationService

I heard there is an improvement in the Setup process, i.e., no more need to manually create the NavigationService destination (outlined in Step B.2.4 of my blog My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the S/4HANA Public Edition (Pt B:Joule Installation). If you continue to add this destination, it won’t hurt because the system is no longer seeking the information stored there. I will remove this step in my blog.

Joule Booster is Faster

The Joule Booster is noticeably faster.  In my case, it is about 15 minutes to complete the running.

SAP Work Zone Content Provider

When I provision the user and group information from the Public Cloud to the Work Zone via IPS, I got an error: The provided providerId is illegal: "status":400,"scimType":"invalidValue".  There is even an KBA addressing this problem 3505749 - The provided providerId is illegal: "status":400,"scimType":"invalidValue".  It turns out not because of my ProviderID is bad, but I only copied part of the ProviderID 6d5e30cb-4 from sap.start.6d5e30cb-4.  I learned that from creating my own ProviderID.  The entire string is the ProviderID, not part of it.  The details count.

Joule Icon Not Shown (4/9/2026)

While installing Joule recently, I found the Joule Icon won't show on the Fiori Launchpad. By further investigation, I learned that from Release 2602, SP 06, Patch 0005, a new authorization is implemented: for Joule icon to be seen, a user needs a business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_UI_JOU_PC.  Here is the background information:

After running Joule Booster, two communication scenarios were created automatically:

  • SAP_COM_0647 for Launchpad Content Exposure to SAP BTP Integration
  • SAP_COM_0882 for Joule Integration

Within SAP_COM_0882, there is one property called Plug-in Activation State. This property controls how the Joule icon shows up or not, or who can use Joule or not.  It has three available values:

  • Empty - Joule is available to all users;
  • DISABLED - Joule is not available to users;
  • ROLE - Joule is only available to those users with the proper authorization, i.e., the users with the business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_UI_JOU_PC.

Property Plug-in Activation State

 With this new parameter, the Administrator can control who can access Joule or not.

The business catalog is part of the SAP_BR_EMPLOYEE business role template. 

If you use a business role copied from the SAP's role template SAP_BR_EMPLOYEE , you need to re-sync it to add this business catalog.

If you don't use this SAP_BR_EMPLOYEE template, just create a new business role to assign the business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_UI_JOU_PC to it. You can name this new business role like "Display Joule Icon", and assign all users to it.

There is an KBA discussing this topic: 3720220 - SAP Joule Icon Does Not Appear in the SAP S4 HANA Cloud Public Edition After Configuration Has Been Completed. For the Public Cloud users, it is recommended to select the value ROLE so that you have control the access to Joule.

 

References

  1. Blog 1: Joule – End-to-End Setup Guide (for all Line of Business) Unified Approach
  2. Blog 2: Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - Setup Guide
  3. Discovery Center 1: Establish a Unified Joule Instance
  4. Discovery Center 2: Activate Joule with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  5. SAP Help: Joule onboarding guide
  6. My Blog: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Part A Prepara...
  7. My Blog: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Part B Joule I...
  8. My Blog: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Part C Joule D...
  9. My Blog: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Part D Improvements)

entry.title: From Insights to Foresight: Elevating Analytics with SAP Analytics Cloud, Joule, BTP and Perplexity
entry.content:
From Insights to Foresight: Elevating Analytics with
SAP Analytics Cloud, Joule, BTP and Perplexity

Introduction

We’ve all been there: you have the internal sales figures, but you’re missing the "why" from the outside world. Is a raw material shortage in Asia spiking your costs? Is a competitor’s new product launch affecting your pipeline?

In today’s fast-paced business environment, having data isn't enough; you need the right data at the right time, regardless of where it lives. Whether it’s a row in a database, a paragraph in a PDF, or a trending market report on the web, the goal is to provide a unified experience.

This post explores how we are extending the standard capabilities of SAP Business Data Cloud using the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and AI Foundation to create massive value. We will make use of a demo scenario in which our famous BestRunBikes company is facing significant declines in the racing bike segment.

 

1. The Core: Master Your Structured Data with Analytical Insights

The journey begins with making structured data accessible to everyone, not just analysts. With Analytical Insights in Joule (released in Q2 2025), users can interact with their business data using natural language. With Joule in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) we bring this analytical power to the fingertips of our SAC users (planned GA see below).

Example: At the Homescreen of SAC I recognize that my order value faces a significant decrease. We jump into the Metrics list to dive deeper. The Smart Insights (as built-in embedded ai capability) indicates that the racing product type is causing the issue. I want to understand which products belong to that type, so i ask Joule to "show me the product sales for racing" which results in an immediate visual chart generated by SAC.

 

Value:

  • Democratized Access: Analytics are available directly from line-of-business applications, saving time and training costs.
  • Powered by "Just Ask": This feature is enabled by SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) and its "just ask" functionality.
  • Deep Integration: It supports both SAC acquired models and SAP Datasphere models, ensuring data-access controls and authorizations remain intact.

Note: The demo scenario shows Joule in SAC, which is a feature that is not yet general available. As of date of the post, the current plan is for Controlled Release with SAC QRC2 2026 & a planned GA with QRC3 2026. The timelines are subject to change as per the usual disclaimers. The experience of Joule itself though can already be achieved with Joule being deployed somewhere else like e.g. Successfactors, S4, Workzone,...

 

2. The Context: Grounding AI in Your Company’s Wisdom

The first step in extending Joule is giving it access to your "dark data"—the unstructured information living in PDFs, images & more. Using the SAP BTP Document Grounding service, we can link Joule to internal sources like SharePoint or Workzone.

Example: In our demo we want to understand if there are certain reasons for the decline in racing bikes. We know that we repeatedly buy market research that lives as pdf in our sharepoint. We understand that there is reduced consumer spending and increased safety concerns from the customers. We could now reach out to the product manager for racing bikes to discuss this.

 

Value:

  • Unstructured Data Integration: Access internal information stored in documents on platforms like SharePoint or Workzone.
  • Internal Knowledge Base: Users can query for internal guidelines, KPI definitions, or existing market research documents.
  • Reliable Foundation: This is an official BTP service, designed to provide "grounding" for AI, ensuring responses are based on your actual business documents.

Have a look at our AI Discovery Center to learn more about this service.

 

3. The Vision: Global Intelligence with Perplexity Integration

The final frontier is connecting your internal world with the external one. By building a custom Joule Skill in Joule Studio, we can tap into the power of Perplexity for real-time web information.

Example: We want to have the latest informations / data around the bike market and specifically look into the european market. We can also ask for certain market projections. With the help of the perplexity skill we can get this kind of information right at the fingertips of the enduser. And now imagine to use this directly as input for simulation & planning!

 

The Architecture Stack

To bring Perplexity into the Joule interface, we utilize three core pillars of the SAP Business Technology Platform:

  • Joule Studio: This is where the Custom Skill is defined and configured. It acts as the front-end logic that tells Joule when to trigger an external search.
  • Cloud Foundry (The CAP Wrapper): We use a Cloud Application Programming (CAP) model wrapper running on Cloud Foundry. This layer handles the API logic, ensuring the data is formatted correctly between the SAP ecosystem and the external provider. Thanks to vibe coding this was minimal effort.
  • AI Foundation (SAP AI Core): The heavy lifting of the AI communication happens here. We use SAP AI Core to securely query Perplexity, providing a robust and governed gateway to the web.

 

Value:

  • External Market Reach: Extend Joule’s information reach to include external market data, projections, and raw material price developments.
  • Technical Synergy: This setup uses SAP AI Core to query Perplexity, acting as a bridge between the enterprise and the live web. It ensures that external queries are handled with enterprise-grade security.
  • Future-Ready: While this can be built today as a custom skill, it is already on the roadmap to become a standard capability of Joule.

 

Conclusion: The Power of an Integrated Platform

By combining the Business Data Cloud (SAC and Datasphere) with the SAP BTP AI Foundation, we aren't just building tools; we are building an intelligent ecosystem. We are turning Joule from an analytical chatbot into a comprehensive business assistant that knows your data, your documents, and the world at large. 

 

Cheers,

Tobias

entry.title: Our 2026 Roadmap for Joule for Developers ABAP AI capabilities
entry.content:
Sapphire 2026 is approaching quickly and as usual it will bring lots of new announcements. So now is the right point in time to recap what has been announced already until Q2/2026 and prepare for the ABAP AI sessions.

The ABAP Platform AI (covering both SAP BTP ABAP Environment and SAP S/4HANA) transitions from a set of independent AI skills to full-scale Agentic AI. The roadmap focuses heavily on developer productivity via Joule and the modernization of ABAP development tools. The key benefits for customers and partners of the roadmap until Q2/2026 are:

 

  1. ABAP development tools in VS Code with ABAP MCP Server.
  2. ABAP AI for lower releases of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition (i.e. 2021, 2022, 2023 and 2025).
  3. Simplified customer on-boarding of ABAP AI via SAP for Me and Central Business Configuration.
  4. Faster innovation with Side-by-Side consumption of SAP and 3rd party Agentic IDEs and LLMs
    (e.g. Microsoft, Amazon, IBM, OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral…)
 

The biggest shift in 2026 will be the move toward Agentic AI, where the existing ABAP AI skills doesn't just explain, complete or generate code (e.g. RAP, CDS, Tests) independently but acts as an autonomous collaborator. This includes built-in ABAP MCP (Model Context Protocol) server. The introduction of VS Code as IDE can only be a start as the depth and breath of Eclipse which we achieved in so many years cannot be reached with one or two steps in VS Code. It'll be a journey which starts with first providing End-to-end UI services development with agents.

The new ABAP AI side-by-side architecture is designed to provide a scalable and future‑proof foundation for ABAP AI capabilities across heterogeneous SAP landscapes. It enables customers to consume ABAP AI services side‑by‑side, independent of the underlying S/4HANA release, ensuring access even for lower‑release Private Edition systems. 

A core benefit of the architecture is also its simplified enablement model through SAP for Me and Central Business Configuration, reducing on-boarding effort and ensuring consistent lifecycle management across environments. 
In addition, the architecture allows us to accelerate innovation speed by allowing to leverage SAP‑provided ABAP AI models alongside leading third‑party LLMs (e.g., Microsoft, Amazon, IBM, OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral) more independently from releases. This flexibility ensures rapid adoption of new capabilities without dependency on individual release cycles.

If you missed the highlights we delivered in Q1/2026 have a look into what Olga Dolinskaja shared about ABAP AI ATC finding explain and custom code explain and proposals. Although this will be a focus also for our future roadmap we will also not stop investing into AI app generators like the Embedded Analytics model generator. 

 
Here you can learn more:
  1. SAP Joule for Developers (J4D) Expands to Private Cloud: Accelerating ABAP Innovation and Transformation
  2. Your Roadmap for getting started with ABAP AI
  3. ABAP AI Revolution Accelerates: The ABAP Developer Who Built Enterprise Apps in Minutes
  4. Joule for Developers and ABAP AI capabilities are coming to SAP S/4HANA Private Edition 2021, 2022, and 2023
  5. Public Roadmap: SAP Help / SAP Roadmap Explorer
  6. Help & Guides
    1. Step-by-step guide how to activate SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities for your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
    2. Joule for Developers (J4D) for S/4HANA Public Cloud Setup Guide
    3. SAP ABAP AI Discovery Center
    4. SAP BTP Discovery Center (Free Tier Services)
    5. Custom code migration to SAP S/4HANA powered by SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities
    6. YouTube playlist for Joule for Developers (J4D)
    7. Joule for Developers Help (J4D)
    8. Build SAP Fiori Apps with ABAP Cloud and SAP Joule for developers (RAP120) 
    9. ADT Eclipse AI Capabilities Help
    10. ABAP AI SDK Help
    11. ABAP-1 Help

More high-level insights about Joule for Developers/ABAP AI and ABAP platform can be found here . Here you find more on ABAP- 1,

entry.title: Optional Task: Use Joule CDS Unit Test Generation
entry.content:
Thank you to everyone who participated in the ABAP Developer Challenge over the past four weeks and successfully completed all four tasks focused on ABAP AI capabilities. We truly appreciate the enthusiasm and engagement shown throughout the challenge. As a follow-up, we are excited to introduce an additional bonus task.

In Week 2, you enhanced a CDS data definition by implementing simple logic. Building on that work, your next step is to create an ABAP test class to validate the logic within the CDS data definition. For this task, you will utilize another feature of Joule’s ABAP AI capabilities — CDS Test Generation.

Task:

  • Select the CDS data definition that you enhanced in Week 2.
  • Generate an ABAP test class using Joule’s CDS Test Generation feature.
  • Execute the test class and ensure that all generated test cases run successfully.

Please refer to the link below for detailed guidance on how to use this ABAP AI capability.

https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-development-tools-user-guide/use-cds-test-class-generator?locale=en-US

entry.title: AI and UX sessions at SAP Sapphire 2026 that I’m most excited about
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire is where SAP makes its big announcements about its vision for the future of ERP and business applications. This includes showing new products, presenting customers who have transformed their business using SAP’s solutions, and highlighting new technologies that will shape the future.

For 2026, AI will continue to dominate the event. AI will change the way that people consume enterprise software through chat-based interfaces and screens that generate on-the-fly based on what you want to do. Eventually.

In this post, I’ll share the sessions at Sapphire and ASUG Annual Conference that I’m most excited about. For the full menu, you can browse the session catalog. My preferences are, of course, shaped by my role on the Cloud ERP UX Foundation team. I’ll organize by date so you can plan your agenda. You can also use the browser to search by topic or speaker if that makes it easier for you. Some are even available on-demand. Let’s go!

Monday, May 11, 2026

Day / Time / Session ID

Session title and description

Speakers

Monday
10am – 5pm
ASUG102

Unlocking Business Value with AI: Strategy, Security, and the Road Ahead

Gain a comprehensive and practical overview of SAP Business AI. This workshop will examine core strategies, security frameworks, and future trends while exploring real-world customer stories. The session will conclude with an expert panel and a hands-on AI road mapping session.

Many experts from SAP

Monday
2:00pm – 2:20pm
ERP2143

The future of enterprise: Innovations in SAP Cloud ERP Private

Discover how SAP Cloud ERP Private solutions enable the next era of enterprise transformation. This introductory session explores how cutting-edge artificial intelligence, trusted data, and integrated applications work in harmony, creating smarter, faster, and more resilient businesses within the security and flexibility of your private cloud.

Nicole Smythe, SAP

Vinay V, SAP

Monday
2:30pm – 2:50pm
CXP2149

Legacy to legendary: The AI-enhanced path

Explore the strategic road map for moving from legacy on-premises systems to a cloud-native, AI-enhanced architecture. Discover how a clean core approach helps reduce technical debt, accelerate innovation, and position your organization to use AI for greater efficiency and agility.

 

Monday
3:30pm – 3:50pm
PUX1349

Innovations and value that SAP Business AI offers

Learn how you can find the best AI features for your use cases, and understand the business value and associated cost with our self-service tools.

Pascal Mauge, SAP

 

Tuesday, May 12, 2026

Day / Time / Session ID

Session title and description

Speakers

Starting with the “My Home” page and drilling down into typical SAP Fiori apps, see how AI makes it easier to find information, enter data, automate tasks, and make informed business decisions. Explore how conversational interactions make SAP solutions more intuitive.

11:30 am - 12:15 pm ERP1622

Why ERP transformations fail: The change management gap

Organizations expect operational efficiency and digital capabilities from their SAP solution transformations, yet some ERP programs fail to realize full value. The missing link is often adoption. Kara Beck, Moog Inc.

1:00 pm - 1:45 pm ERP1864

Sophia Levens, SAP

11:30 am – 12:15 pm ERP2238

Western Sugar’s success with SAP Cloud ERP

Five years after go-live, Western Sugar Cooperative calls SAP Cloud ERP applications “wonderful.” Hear about why it chose public cloud; the value of clean core, best practices, strong configuration and integration, and continuous improvement; its smooth move from a two- to three-system landscape; and readiness for AI-enabled innovation.

 

Wednesday
11:30 am – 12:15 pm FOU2337

When AI becomes the interface to the enterprise

For decades, SAP helped shape enterprise software based on screens and transactions. Today, AI is evolving how we interact with systems. Explore how SAP leads this shift—uniting applications, data, and AI—and what this next phase of transformation means for organizations turning AI into a real competitive advantage.

Florian Heretsch, SAP

Wednesday
1:00 pm – 1:45 pm FOU1557

Getting started with SAP Build MCPs: Create your first CAP app and process

 Experience the powerful Model Context Protocol (MCP) integration in SAP Build solutions. Produce your first SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) application and SAP Fiori elements UI, while orchestrating workflows. See how AI-enhanced context improves coding efficiency and helps you build scalable, process-rich apps.

 

Tim Back, SAP

Jason Gabor, SAP

Wednesday
1:00 pm – 1:45 pm BTM1232

The AI-augmented architect: Upgrading architecture in the age of AI 

 AI is reshaping enterprise architecture. To provide value, enterprise architectures must embrace AI tools. Discover SAP technology leveraging AI for enhanced visibility and coordination. Through real-world examples, see how Joule and agentic AI increase information velocity and augment architect capabilities to thrive in this evolving landscape.

Martin Mysyk, SAP

2:00 pm – 2:20 pm PUX1210

AI and the future of user experience in SAP software

Prepare yourself for the upcoming revolution in user experience by understanding how we are harnessing AI to help users achieve better outcomes. Discover how AI is changing the idea of what an application is—and what this means for you. Learn about our AI design patterns and get a glimpse of the future SAP software user experience.
